cards.front: Where is genetic material enclosed in plant and animal cells?
cards.explanation: Nuclear enclosure distinguishes these eukaryotic cells from prokaryotic bacterial cells.

choices.feedback: Plant and animal cells have their genetic material enclosed in a nucleus.
A single DNA loop is the genetic material arrangement in bacterial cells.
choices.misconception: Applies the bacterial single-DNA-loop arrangement to eukaryotic cells.

choices.feedback: Plasmids are small DNA rings that may occur in bacterial cells, while plant and animal genetic material is enclosed in a nucleus.
choices.misconception: Applies possible bacterial plasmids to the location of eukaryotic genetic material.

choices.feedback: Bacterial genetic material is not enclosed in a nucleus; plant and animal genetic material is.
choices.misconception: Applies the prokaryotic lack of nuclear enclosure to eukaryotic cells.

evidence.sourceExcerpt: Plant and animal cells (eukaryotic cells) have a cell membrane, cytoplasm and genetic material enclosed in a nucleus. Bacterial cells (prokaryotic cells) are much smaller in comparison. They have cytoplasm and a cell membrane surrounded by a cell wall. The genetic material is not enclosed in a nucleus. It is a single DNA loop and there may be one or more small rings of DNA called plasmids.

cards.front: What form does the bacterial cell's genetic material take?
cards.explanation: Small DNA rings called plasmids may also occur in the cell.

choices.feedback: The bacterial cell's genetic material is a single DNA loop.

choices.text: Genetic material enclosed in a nucleus.
choices.feedback: Plant and animal cells enclose genetic material in a nucleus; bacterial cells do not.
choices.misconception: Transfers the eukaryotic nuclear arrangement to a prokaryotic cell.

choices.feedback: Plasmids are possible small DNA rings; bacterial genetic material is stated as a single DNA loop.
choices.misconception: Treats possible plasmids as the only bacterial genetic material.

choices.text: A cell membrane surrounded by a cell wall.
choices.feedback: The membrane and surrounding wall are bacterial cell structures; the genetic material is stated separately as a single DNA loop.
choices.misconception: Confuses the membrane and cell wall with bacterial genetic material.

evidence.sourceExcerpt: Plant and animal cells (eukaryotic cells) have a cell membrane, cytoplasm and genetic material enclosed in a nucleus. Bacterial cells (prokaryotic cells) are much smaller in comparison. They have cytoplasm and a cell membrane surrounded by a cell wall. The genetic material is not enclosed in a nucleus. It is a single DNA loop and there may be one or more small rings of DNA called plasmids.

cards.front: What fills the permanent vacuole often found in plant cells?
cards.explanation: This vacuole is an additional part often present in plant cells.

choices.feedback: The permanent vacuole in plant cells is filled with cell sap.
Cytoplasm is listed as a cell part; cell sap fills the permanent vacuole.
choices.misconception: Confuses cytoplasm with the contents of the permanent vacuole.

choices.feedback: Cellulose makes the plant and algal cell wall, not the vacuole's contents.
choices.misconception: Confuses cell-wall material with vacuole contents.

choices.feedback: Chloroplasts are plant-cell parts alongside the vacuole; they do not fill it.
choices.misconception: Confuses another plant-cell structure with vacuole contents.

evidence.sourceExcerpt: Most animal cells have the following parts: • a nucleus • cytoplasm • a cell membrane • mitochondria • ribosomes. In addition to the parts found in animal cells, plant cells often have: • chloroplasts • a permanent vacuole filled with cell sap. Plant and algal cells also have a cell wall made of cellulose, which strengthens the cell.

cards.front: What are plant and algal cell walls made of?
cards.explanation: The cellulose cell wall strengthens the cell.

choices.feedback: Plant and algal cells have a cell wall made of cellulose.
Cell sap fills the permanent vacuole; the cell wall is made of cellulose.
choices.misconception: Confuses vacuole contents with cell-wall material.

choices.feedback: A cell membrane is listed separately as a cell part; the plant and algal cell wall is made of cellulose.
choices.misconception: Treats the cell membrane as the material forming the cell wall.

choices.feedback: Cytoplasm is listed as a cell part, whereas the plant and algal cell wall is made of cellulose.
choices.misconception: Confuses cell contents with cell-wall material.

evidence.sourceExcerpt: Most animal cells have the following parts: • a nucleus • cytoplasm • a cell membrane • mitochondria • ribosomes. In addition to the parts found in animal cells, plant cells often have: • chloroplasts • a permanent vacuole filled with cell sap. Plant and algal cells also have a cell wall made of cellulose, which strengthens the cell.

cards.front: When do most types of animal cells differentiate?
cards.explanation: Many plant cell types instead retain the ability to differentiate throughout life.

choices.feedback: Most types of animal cell differentiate at an early stage.
Many plant cell types retain differentiation ability throughout life; most animal cell types differentiate early.
choices.misconception: Transfers the lifelong differentiation pattern of many plant cells to animal cells.

choices.feedback: In mature animals, cell division is mainly restricted to repair and replacement; most animal cell types differentiate early.
choices.misconception: Confuses mature-animal cell division with the timing of differentiation.

choices.feedback: A cell becomes specialised as it differentiates and acquires different sub-cellular structures.
choices.misconception: Reverses the relationship between differentiation and specialisation.

evidence.sourceExcerpt: As an organism develops, cells differentiate to form different types of cells. • Most types of animal cell differentiate at an early stage. • Many types of plant cells retain the ability to differentiate throughout life. In mature animals, cell division is mainly restricted to repair and replacement. As a cell differentiates it acquires different sub-cellular structures to enable it to carry out a certain function. It has become a specialised cell.

cards.front: What is cell division mainly restricted to in mature animals?
cards.explanation: This differs from differentiation, which most animal cell types undergo at an early stage.

choices.feedback: In mature animals, cell division is mainly restricted to repair and replacement.
Many plant cells retain the ability to differentiate throughout life; mature-animal cell division is mainly for repair and replacement.
choices.misconception: Transfers a plant-cell differentiation ability to mature-animal cell division.

choices.feedback: Differentiation forms different cell types as an organism develops; mature-animal cell division is mainly restricted to repair and replacement.
choices.misconception: Confuses the outcome of differentiation with the main role of division in mature animals.

choices.feedback: A cell acquires different sub-cellular structures as it differentiates, not as the stated restriction on mature-animal cell division.
choices.misconception: Confuses structural changes during differentiation with mature-animal cell division.

evidence.sourceExcerpt: As an organism develops, cells differentiate to form different types of cells. • Most types of animal cell differentiate at an early stage. • Many types of plant cells retain the ability to differentiate throughout life. In mature animals, cell division is mainly restricted to repair and replacement. As a cell differentiates it acquires different sub-cellular structures to enable it to carry out a certain function. It has become a specialised cell.

cards.front: What does a cell acquire as it differentiates for a certain function?
cards.explanation: Acquiring these structures makes the cell specialised.

choices.feedback: A differentiating cell acquires different sub-cellular structures to carry out a certain function.
Differentiation forms different cell types, but an individual differentiating cell acquires different sub-cellular structures.
choices.misconception: Confuses the result of differentiation across cells with what one cell acquires.

choices.feedback: Repair and replacement restrict cell division in mature animals; they are not structures acquired during differentiation.
choices.misconception: Confuses the role of mature-animal cell division with structural change during differentiation.

choices.text: The ability to differentiate throughout life.
choices.feedback: Many plant cells retain this ability throughout life; it is not what a differentiating cell acquires.
choices.misconception: Confuses a retained plant-cell ability with structures acquired during differentiation.

evidence.sourceExcerpt: As an organism develops, cells differentiate to form different types of cells. • Most types of animal cell differentiate at an early stage. • Many types of plant cells retain the ability to differentiate throughout life. In mature animals, cell division is mainly restricted to repair and replacement. As a cell differentiates it acquires different sub-cellular structures to enable it to carry out a certain function. It has become a specialised cell.

cards.front: How does an electron microscope compare with a light microscope?
cards.back: It has much higher magnification and resolving power.
cards.explanation: This allows cells to be studied in much finer detail.

choices.text: It has much higher magnification and resolving power.
choices.feedback: Both magnification and resolving power are much higher in an electron microscope.

choices.text: It has higher magnification but lower resolving power.
choices.feedback: Both magnification and resolving power are much higher, rather than resolving power being lower.
choices.misconception: Assumes greater magnification comes with lower resolving power.

choices.text: It has lower magnification but higher resolving power.
choices.feedback: Both magnification and resolving power are much higher, rather than magnification being lower.
choices.misconception: Assumes greater resolving power comes with lower magnification.

choices.text: It has the same magnification and resolving power.
choices.feedback: An electron microscope has much higher magnification and resolving power than a light microscope.
choices.misconception: Assumes electron and light microscopes have equal capabilities.

evidence.sourceExcerpt: An electron microscope has much higher magnification and resolving power than a light microscope. This means that it can be used to study cells in much finer detail. This has enabled biologists to see and understand many more sub-cellular structures.
